texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We construct Wong-Zakai approximations of time-inhomogeneous stochastic differential equations with regime switching (RSSDEs), and provide a convergence rate. In the proposed approximations, the standard Brownian motion driving the time-inhomogeneous RSSDEs is replaced by a family of finitevariation processes {F λ } λ> 0 . We show that if F λ strongly converges to B at rate δ ( λ ) , then the Wong-Zakai approximation strongly converges to the original solution of the time-inhomogeneous RSSDE at rate δ ( λ ) λ ε , for any ε > 0 . This is the first paper on Wong-Zakai approximations for time-inhomogeneous RSSDEs, and significantly extends the counterparts for time-homogeneous SDEs without regime switching in Römisch and Wakolbinger [24].
texts.text: We construct Wong-Zakai approximations of time-inhomogeneous stochastic differential equations with regime switching (RSSDEs), and provide a convergence rate. In the proposed approximations, the standard Brownian motion driving the time-inhomogeneous RSSDEs is replaced by a family of finitevariation processes {F λ } λ> 0 . We show that if F λ strongly converges to B at rate δ ( λ ) , then the Wong-Zakai approximation strongly converges to the original solution of the time-inhomogeneous RSSDE at rate δ ( λ ) λ ε , for any ε > 0 . This is the first paper on Wong-Zakai approximations for time-inhomogeneous RSSDEs, and significantly extends the counterparts for time-homogeneous SDEs without regime switching in Römisch and Wakolbinger [24].

texts.orig: Details of Assumptions 1-4 can be found in later sections. Briefly speaking, Assumption 1 is about linear growth and local Lipschitz continuity, and is standard for ensuring existence and uniqueness of solutions of SDEs. Assumption 2 provides bounds for the diffusion coefficient σ and its derivative, and is standard for Wong-Zakai approximations. Assumption 3 is needed specifically because of the time-inhomogeneity feature, and does not automatically follow from the properties of µ and σ described in Assumptions 1 and 2. Assumption 4 arises due to the regime switching feature, and bounds the tail of the jump distribution of J over a compact time interval. This assumption allows us to obtain the convergence rate in the regime-switching case, without being overly restrictive: it holds whenever J has a bounded jump intensity. Cases where Assumption 4 is satisfied include, but are not limited to, time-homogeneous Markovian, time-inhomogeneous Markovian, and semi-Markovian processes.

texts.orig: Most related to this paper is the work of Römisch and Wakolbinger [24], which determined the rate of strong convergence of the Wong-Zakai approximation for time-homogeneous SDEs without regime switching, under Assumptions 1 and 2 reduced to the time-homogeneous setting. While our strong approximation is an extension of [24], our methods significantly differ from theirs in order to handle challenges that the regime-switching and time-inhomongeneity generalizations bring to the problem. A key technique in our analysis is applying the Lamperti transform [16] to regime-switching SDEs (1.1). This was previously applied in Pettersson [22] and Zhang [32] in the simpler case of time-homogeneous SDEs without regime switching. The Lamperti transformation leads to clear expressions in the approximation and an insight on how to bound approximation errors at jump epochs of J .

texts.orig: and references therein. We describe briefly three major directions of generalization of Wong-Zakai results. First, the Wong-Zakai framework has been extended to SDEs driven by higher-dimensional Brownian motion processes. Stroock and Varadhan [26] proposed piecewise-linear approximations and showed convergence in probability; Ikeda et al. [13] and Ikeda and Watanabe [14] proved, respectively, convergence in meansquare and uniform convergence over finite time interval. Second, Wong-Zakai approximations have also been obtained for reflected SDEs. Pettersson [22] analysed SDEs reflected on the boundary of a convex set, replacing the Brownian motion process with its polygonal approximation, and obtained uniform convergence. Evans and Stroock [6] approximated SDEs reflected in general domains and obtained convergence in law. Zhang [31] showed strong convergence of reflected SDE in multi-dimensional general domains. Third, Wong-Zakai approximations have been generalized to stochastic partial differential equations, such as the Wong-Zakai theorem obtained by Hairer and Pardoux [12] for one-dimensional parabolic nonlinear stochastic PDEs driven by space-time white noise.
texts.text: and references therein. We describe briefly three major directions of generalization of Wong-Zakai results. First, the Wong-Zakai framework has been extended to SDEs driven by higher-dimensional Brownian motion processes. Stroock and Varadhan [26] proposed piecewise-linear approximations and showed convergence in probability; Ikeda et al. [13] and Ikeda and Watanabe [14] proved, respectively, convergence in meansquare and uniform convergence over finite time interval. Second, Wong-Zakai approximations have also been obtained for reflected SDEs. Pettersson [22] analysed SDEs reflected on the boundary of a convex set, replacing the Brownian motion process with its polygonal approximation, and obtained uniform convergence. Evans and Stroock [6] approximated SDEs reflected in general domains and obtained convergence in law. Zhang [31] showed strong convergence of reflected SDE in multi-dimensional general domains. Third, Wong-Zakai approximations have been generalized to stochastic partial differential equations, such as the Wong-Zakai theorem obtained by Hairer and Pardoux [12] for one-dimensional parabolic nonlinear stochastic PDEs driven by space-time white noise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results on convergence rates in Wong-Zakai approximations are much rarer; we briefly highlight a few. Brzeniak and Flandoli [3] proved almost sure convergence for parabolic and hyperbolic evolution equations and provided estimates for the rate of convergence. Later, Brzeniak and Carroll [2] obtained convergence rate for approximations of SDEs in M-type 2 Banach spaces. More recently, Nakayama and Tappe [19] obtained convergence rate for semilinear stochastic partial differential equation, and Ammou and Lanconelli [1] for Stratonovich and Itô quasi-linear SDEs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We outline in Section 2 some results of stochastic calculus for regime-switching processes which will be useful later. In 3.1, we introduce the Lamperti transformation, L , of a solution of a regime-switching SDE, X , and prove its properties. In 3.2, we construct the transformation pathwise and prove its uniqueness. In Section 4, we apply the Lamperti transformation to the approximating process X λ , given in (1.2), to obtain L λ and compute its convergence rate to L . We show that, in fact, L λ is the unique càdlàg solution to a regime-switching SDE driven by F λ with jumps . Lipschitz continuity helps us obtain the almost sure convergence, and subsequently the convergence rate, from X λ to X , giving rise to Theorem 1.1. Finally, in Section 6 we state a direct implication of Theorem 1.1, with respect to recent approximations of Markov-modulated Brownian motion [17, 21].

This follows by choosing a sufficiently large c > 0 such that each holding time of J stochastically dominates an exponential variable with rate c , implying that N ∗ ∼ Poisson ( c ) stochastically dominates N . Thus, Lemma 4.3 allows us to work with jump processes that are not necessarily time-homogeneous Markovian, such as time-inhomogeneous Markovian or semi-Markovian processes.

texts.text: Proof. The bijectivity of f γ,q follows from the fact that it is strictly increasing, with lim x →∞ f γ,q ( x ) = ∞ . Let y be in the image of f γ,q , so that it is of the form
